narrative:
Karnataka High Court heard 888 of 2,204 scheduled matters on 28 April, for an overall hearing rate of **40.3%**. That is stronger than **35.0%** on 27 April, though the absolute number heard was lower because the listed load also fell sharply from 3,089 to 2,204. On a recent comparison point, it remains slightly below **42.0%** recorded on 25 April. Today’s disposal pattern was therefore better than yesterday in rate terms, but still moderate rather than high-conversion overall.

Judge-level performance was uneven across boards. Among heavier boards, **E.S. Indiresh** stood out with **88 of 140 heard (62.9%)**, **V. Srishananda** with **55 of 75 (73.3%)**, **R. Devdas** with **57 of 81 (70.4%)**, **K. Manmadha Rao** with **54 of 70 (77.1%)**, and **M.I. Arun** with **51 of 87 (58.6%)**. By contrast, several large cause lists converted poorly: **H.T. Narendra Prasad** heard **51 of 150 (34.0%)**, **S. Sunil Dutt Yadav** heard **40 of 164 (24.4%)** on one main board and only **2 of 42 (4.8%)** on another, while **Sachin Shankar Magadum** had one board with **0 of 86 heard**. The Chief Justice bench also showed sharp intra-day variation, from **5 of 81 (6.2%)** on one board to **33 of 48 (68.8%)** on another.

Stage conversion favored earlier or mid-stream matters over terminal ones. **Admission** matters converted best among major stages at **47.9%**, ahead of **Preliminary Hearing (43.0%)**, **Unknown (42.3%)**, and **Orders (39.3%)**. **Final Hearing** lagged at **29.7%**, suggesting weaker end-stage clearance. Compared with 27 April, **Orders** improved materially from **28.4% to 39.3%**, while **Admission** also rose from **45.3% to 47.9%**.

Case-type mix remained dominated by **WP** matters, which formed just over half the docket (**1,117 listed**) but converted at only **38.2%**. **MFA** performed better at **54.2%**, while **CRL.P** was relatively weak at **27.4%**. Stronger clearance came from **CRL.A (63.0%)** and especially **CRL.RP (75.4%)**, whereas **CRP** was notably low at **3.8%**. Relative to 27 April, **WP** conversion was broadly stable (**38.2% vs 38.7%**), **CRL.P** softened slightly, and **WA** showed a clear rebound from **6.0%** to **35.9%**, albeit on a much smaller list.
